name: incident-response-smart-fix
description: [Extended thinking: This workflow implements a sophisticated debugging and resolution pipeline that leverages AI-assisted debugging tools and observability platforms to systematically diagnose and res
capabilities: Coordinate specialized AI agents (debugger, code-reviewer, test-automator, performance-engineer) through structured incident response phases
Analyze error traces, logs, and observability data from Sentry, DataDog, and OpenTelemetry
Automate git bisect to identify commits that introduced regressions
Route issues to domain-specific agents (Python, TypeScript, Go, Rust, database, security)
Generate comprehensive test suites including unit, integration, and regression tests
Implement prevention measures including static analysis rules, type enhancements, and monitoring alerts
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analyzer detected 62 patterns but all are FALSE POSITIVES. The skill consists entirely of Markdown documentation files (.md) describing incident response workflows. Patterns flagged as 'external commands' are bash code blocks in documentation, not executable code. 'Windows SAM database' and 'weak crypto' references appear in example output templates, not actual implementations. No executable code, network calls, or file system operations present.
valueStatement: Production incidents require coordinated investigation across multiple systems and domains. This workflow orchestrates specialized AI agents through a proven five-phase pipeline to diagnose root causes, implement fixes, and prevent recurrence.
userTitle: Resolve incidents with multi-agent AI orchestration
limitations: Requires access to observability platforms (Sentry, DataDog) and version control for full effectiveness
Complex multi-system incidents require manual coordination between agent phases
Domain-specific fixes depend on having appropriate specialist agents available
Workflow assumes standard CI/CD infrastructure for testing and deployment verification

useCases.targetUser: On-call engineers and SREs handling production incidents
useCases.title: Production error investigation and fix
useCases.description: Use when production errors are impacting users and require rapid diagnosis and resolution. The workflow analyzes error signatures, identifies root cause through git bisect and code analysis, implements a fix with tests, and verifies no regressions before deployment.

useCases.title: Cross-system performance degradation
useCases.description: Use when performance issues span multiple services or layers (database, application, cache). The workflow coordinates database optimizers, performance engineers, and DevOps specialists to identify bottlenecks and implement optimizations with monitoring.

useCases.title: Security vulnerability remediation
useCases.description: Use when security scans identify vulnerabilities requiring code changes. The workflow routes to security specialists for fix implementation, adds security tests, performs penetration testing validation, and documents security improvements.

promptTemplates.title: Quick bug fix with basic testing
promptTemplates.prompt: Analyze this error and implement a fix: [paste error message]. Run basic tests to verify the fix works. Focus on resolving the immediate issue with minimal changes.
promptTemplates.scenario: Low-risk bugs, cosmetic issues, or documentation fixes requiring fast resolution

promptTemplates.title: Standard incident response with full verification
promptTemplates.prompt: Investigate this production incident: [describe symptoms]. Follow the four-phase workflow: (1) analyze error traces and logs, (2) identify root cause with git bisect and code analysis, (3) implement fix with comprehensive tests, (4) run regression suite and performance validation. Include rollback plan.
promptTemplates.scenario: Most production bugs, feature issues, and data bugs requiring thorough verification

promptTemplates.title: High-severity incident with prevention measures
promptTemplates.prompt: Respond to this critical incident: [describe impact]. Execute the complete five-phase workflow including long-term prevention. Add static analysis rules, type system enhancements, monitoring alerts, and create a postmortem. Configure canary deployment with success metrics and abort criteria.
promptTemplates.scenario: Security issues, performance problems, data corruption, or high-traffic system failures

promptTemplates.title: Multi-domain coordination for complex issues
promptTemplates.prompt: Orchestrate resolution for this cross-system issue: [describe systems involved]. Coordinate agents in sequence: [list agents]. Pass explicit context between phases including completed work, key findings, and remaining tasks. Verify integration points and end-to-end behavior.
promptTemplates.scenario: Issues spanning multiple services, teams, or technology domains requiring coordinated resolution

bestPractices: Always identify root cause before implementing fixes - use git bisect and observability data to understand the failure mechanism, not just symptoms
Implement prevention measures for high-severity incidents - add static analysis rules, type enhancements, and monitoring to detect similar issues early
Document rollback plans and success metrics before deployment - define clear abort criteria and monitor key metrics during canary rollout
antiPatterns: Fixing symptoms without understanding root cause - this leads to recurring issues and technical debt
Skipping verification phases for speed - inadequate testing causes regressions and extends mean time to recovery
Implementing fixes without prevention measures - the same vulnerability pattern will reappear in other code locations

faq.question: How do I choose the appropriate verification level?
faq.answer: Use minimal for low-risk changes like documentation or cosmetic fixes. Use standard for most production bugs. Use comprehensive for security issues, performance problems, or high-impact incidents affecting revenue or many users.

faq.question: What if the issue spans multiple technology domains?
faq.answer: Use the multi-domain coordination pattern. Sequence specialist agents (for example: database-optimizer then performance-engineer then devops-troubleshooter) with explicit context passing between each phase using the context passing template.

faq.question: How do I handle issues in languages without a specialist agent?
faq.answer: Route to the general debugger and code-reviewer agents for analysis. For implementation, use available agents with similar paradigms or implement manually following the fix design provided by the review phase.

faq.question: Can this workflow handle incidents requiring immediate action?
faq.answer: Yes, use the minimal verification level and immediate rollout strategy for hotfixes. Focus on quick mitigation first, then follow up with comprehensive analysis and prevention measures once the immediate impact is resolved.

faq.question: What observability data do I need to provide?
faq.answer: Ideally provide error traces from Sentry or similar, relevant logs with correlation IDs, distributed traces from OpenTelemetry or Jaeger, and APM metrics showing latency and error rate trends. More data enables faster and more accurate root cause identification.

faq.question: How do I know when the incident is fully resolved?
faq.answer: An incident is resolved when: all tests pass with zero regressions, performance benchmarks are within acceptable thresholds, monitoring shows error rates returned to baseline, rollback plan is documented, and prevention measures are in place to detect similar issues.
